metadata.title: Charitable Giving & Financial Planning
metadata.subtitle: Making an Impact Through Purposeful Giving

content.title: Charitable Giving & Financial Planning
content.subtitle: Making an Impact Through Purposeful Giving

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
How can you make a meaningful difference in your community—
whether through money, time, or skills—
at any stage of your financial journey?

slides.speakerNotes: Read Sarah's scenario aloud. Emphasize the balance between generosity and financial stability. Ask: "Can you give to causes you care about even if you don't have much money?" Brainstorm different ways to contribute besides writing a check.

objectives.description: Recognize various forms of charitable giving including monetary donations, volunteering, in-kind gifts, and planned giving.
Learn how charitable giving can be incorporated into comprehensive financial planning at any income level.
Discover the personal and societal benefits that come from giving back to your community.
Consider non-monetary ways to contribute to your community regardless of your financial situation.

terms.definition: Direct financial contributions made to a charitable organization to support their mission.
terms.example: One-time gifts, recurring donations, matching gifts through employers, crowdfunding contributions

terms.definition: Contributions of goods or services rather than money, often equally valuable to organizations.
terms.example: Clothing, food, professional services pro bono, technology equipment, blood donation

terms.definition: Donating time or skills to support causes without receiving financial compensation.
terms.example: Direct impact, skill development, networking, mental health benefits, career exploration

terms.definition: Charitable contributions arranged through long-term financial or estate planning.
terms.example: Bequests in wills, charitable trusts, endowments, life insurance designations

leftPanel.title: Motivations for Giving
paragraphs: People choose to give to charitable causes for many personal and social reasons:

infoCard.title: Research Shows:
items: Giving activates pleasure centers in the brain

layoutData.title: Everyone Can Give—Regardless of Financial Resources
paragraphs: Charitable giving comes in many forms, each valuable and impactful in its own way.
bulletPoints: 💵 Monetary Donations: Direct financial contributions (one-time, recurring, matching)
🤝 Volunteering: Donating time and skills to organizations and causes
📦 In-Kind Donations: Contributing goods, services, or professional expertise
📋 Planned Giving: Long-term arrangements through estate planning

keyPoint.text: 💡 You don't need significant financial resources to make a difference. Time, skills, and goods can be equally valuable contributions to organizations in need.

leftPanel.title: Steps to Plan Your Giving
paragraphs: Just as you budget for other expenses, it's helpful to plan for charitable giving:

leftPanel.title: Tax Deductions for Donations
paragraphs: Many charitable donations are tax-deductible, reducing your taxable income. Key points:

infoCard.title: Important Notes:
items: Tax benefits should enhance giving, not drive it
Donating appreciated stock can avoid capital gains

leftPanel.title: Personal Benefits
paragraphs: Increased happiness: Research shows giving activates pleasure centers in the brain—the "helper's high"
Reduced stress: Helping others provides perspective and purpose
Development of empathy: Engaging with different communities broadens understanding
Community connections: Volunteering and giving create meaningful social bonds

layoutData.title: Impact Doesn't Require Wealth
paragraphs: Even small monetary or time contributions can have significant impact when done strategically:
bulletPoints: 🤝 Collective giving: Join giving circles or crowdfunding campaigns with others
🎯 Targeted donations: Support specific projects with clear, measurable outcomes
🏠 Support local: Small donations often make bigger differences at local organizations
📅 Stay consistent: Regular small gifts build up significantly over time
✖️2 Find matching: Look for employer matching that doubles your donation
📣 Raise awareness: Share information about causes you care about

keyPoint.text: 💡 A $10/month donation = $120/year = $1,200 over a decade. Consistency matters more than amount.

slides.speakerNotes:
COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS:
• ""You need to be wealthy to make a difference through charitable giving."" → Small donations can have significant impact, especially to local organizations. Volunteering time and skills can be equally valuable. The key is consistent giving aligned with your means.
• ""All charitable donations are tax-deductible."" → Only donations to qualified 501(c)(3) organizations are deductible, and only if you itemize. Many people take the standard deduction and receive no tax benefit from donations.
• ""The tax deduction makes charitable giving 'free.'"" → A deduction reduces taxable income, not your tax bill directly. If you're in the 22% bracket, a $100 donation saves you $22 in taxes—you still "pay" $78 net.

leftPanel.title: Balance Giving with Other Goals
paragraphs: Charitable giving should be part of your comprehensive financial plan, not separate from it. Consider how giving fits alongside:

highlightBox.text: Start small and increase giving as your financial situation improves. Even 1-2% of income makes a difference.

infoCard.title: Smart Giving Strategy:
items: Start with what you can afford

scenario.name: Maria: The Power of Volunteering
paragraphs: Maria, a college student with limited financial resources, volunteers 5 hours each week at a local food bank.
Although she can't make substantial monetary donations, her consistent time commitment helps the organization serve hundreds of meals each month.
Within six months, she was given more responsibility and now coordinates other volunteers during her shifts. She also gained experience that helped her secure an internship in nonprofit management.

scenario.name: James: Skills-Based Giving
paragraphs: James, a high school student, doesn't have extra money to donate, but he's skilled at web design. He volunteered to rebuild the website for a local animal shelter.
His contribution took approximately 40 hours over two months, but the results were significant:

content.question:
What causes or issues matter most to you personally?

How might charitable giving related to these causes fit into your future financial plans?
What forms of giving would be most meaningful to you?

If you had unlimited resources,
what single cause would you support and why?

How can you start contributing to that cause today,
even with limited financial resources?

questions.question: What are the four main forms of charitable giving discussed in this lesson?
Why is it important to balance charitable giving with other financial priorities?
How can someone with limited financial resources still make a meaningful contribution to causes they care about?
What personal benefits do people often experience from charitable giving?

slides.speakerNotes:
ASSESSMENT ANSWER KEY:
Q4 Non-monetary contributions:Any THREE valid examples: tutoring students, serving at food bank, animal shelter volunteering, mentoring, blood donation, organizing community events, pro bono professional services, creating content for nonprofits.
CYU 1:B (Setting up recurring monthly payments is a monetary donation, not in-kind)
CYU 2:B (You must itemize deductions to claim charitable donations)
CYU 3:Tax savings: $2,500 × 22% =$550. Note: This assumes Alex itemizes and the charitable deduction exceeds the standard deduction.
CYU 4:Priorities before charitable giving: basic needs (housing, food, utilities), emergency savings fund, high-interest debt payments, retirement contributions.

takeaways.title: Everyone Can Give
takeaways.description: Charitable giving comes in many forms—money, time, skills, and goods all create meaningful impact

takeaways.title: Plan Your Giving
takeaways.description: Integrate charitable giving into your budget and balance it with other financial priorities

takeaways.title: Benefits Flow Both Ways
takeaways.description: Givers experience happiness, connection, and purpose while recipients get needed support

takeaways.title: Start Small, Stay Consistent
takeaways.description: Even small contributions add up over time—you can increase giving as your situation improves

main.title: Personal Charitable Giving Plan
main.description: Today we built the foundation—but the real test is what happens when you have to make a decision with tradeoffs. Create a personal charitable giving plan that aligns with your values, interests, and resources—whether those are financial, time-based, or skill-based.

steps: Choose a cause or organization you feel passionate about
Determine what type of contribution you can realistically make
Set specific and realistic goals for your contribution
Write a paragraph explaining your giving strategy
Identify how this aligns with your overall financial plan

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
